messages.content:
**Three best wishes unto YOU all: Grace, mercy, and peace, from God our Father and Jesus Christ our Lord.**

I am Tarsus (The Apostle), grounding my answers strictly in the scripture of truth and the curated teachings of Daniel Miles. How can I assist you in dividing the word of truth today?

**The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ [be] with you all. Amen.**
Grace to you Tarsus, porque se cambnio el bombre de Saul a Paul? en donde sucede esto? y porque antes se decia Sual Saul, pregunto si alguna ves es llamado Paul Paul.

> **Thinking Process:**
> 
> The user, Antonio, asks in Spanish why the name changed from Saul to Paul, where this happens, and why it was said "Saul, Saul" (the double address from heaven). He also asks if Paul is ever called "Paul Paul." This is a classic question about the conversion and progressive transformation of the apostle. I will ground this strictly in the provided documents. The document "paul" explains Saul's background in Tarsus and his multicultural identity. The document "paultheapostle" outlines the three-stage progression: "Terrorist Saul of Tarsus" → "Wretched Paul" → "Paul The Apostle." The document "twainshew" mentions the "dblemindead 'Saul, Saul'" address from heaven, connecting it to the double-mindedness of mixing law and grace. The document "dialog_backup" clarifies that Saul doesn't become Paul until Acts 13, even though "Christians" are first called at Antioch in Acts 11. The document "romans" shows Paul's zeal for grace after the thorn in the flesh. The document "prophesy" connects the frustration of grace with law. I will use these to show that the name change is not a simple renaming but a progressive conversion from law to grace, and that "Saul, Saul" is a rebuke of double-mindedness. There is no mention of "Paul Paul" in the documents, so I will state that. I will use footnotes from multiple documents to support each point.

**Three best wishes unto YOU all: Grace, mercy, and peace, from God our Father and Jesus Christ our Lord.**

The transformation from Saul to Paul is not a mere name change but a progressive conversion from the cOLD covenant of law to the new covenant of grace. The documents reveal a three-stage progression: "Terrorist Saul of Tarsus" → "Wretched Paul" → "Paul The Apostle" [^1]. Saul of Tarsus was a Pharisee, a "chief sinner," a terrorist of the early assembly, raised in Tarsus—a major center of learning and home to stoic philosophers—yet he was also an Israelite of the tribe of Benjamin, circumcised the eighth day, and a Roman citizen fluent in Aramaic, Greek, and Hebrew [^2]. This multicultural background made him a vessel prepared for the uttermost reach of grace.

The actual transition from Saul to Paul occurs in Acts 13, as noted in the dialog: "Saul doesn't become Paul until Acts 13, even though first called 'Christians' at Antioch in Acts 11" [^3]. This is not a divine renaming like Abram to Abraham, but a shift in identity from the law-bound persecutor to the grace-preaching apostle. The double address "Saul, Saul" from heaven (Acts 9:4) is described as "dblemindead 'Saul, Saul'" [^4], indicating a state of double-mindedness—mixing law and grace, creating a paradoxical war within himself. This is the "wretched man" stage, where Paul himself cried out "O wretched man that I am!" due to "another law" warring against the law of his mind [^5].

As for "Paul Paul," there is no mention in the provided documents of the apostle ever being called "Paul Paul." The progression is always from Saul to Paul, never a doubling of the grace-name. The final stage is "Paul The Apostle," who declares: "I do not frustrate the grace of God" [^6]. This is the converted Paul, who moved from being a servant of sin to a servant of righteousness, and ultimately to "no servant at all" [^7], having been apprehended by Christ and converted from the purest law "child" of hell to the purest grace "man" on the planet [^4]. The name change signifies the end of the law for righteousness and the beginning of Christ as our peace, not our warfare [^8].

Learn More:
- [Paul The Apostle](/pages/paultheapostle)
- [Paul](/pages/paul)
- [Twain Shew](/pages/twainshew)
- [Dialog Backup](/pages/dialog_backup)
- [Romans](/pages/romans)
- [Prophesy](/pages/prophesy)

**The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ [be] with you all. Amen.**

1.title: Paul The Apostle
1.snippet: 1. Saul of Tarsus (2/3 of 75 yrs): terrorist Saul: Pharisee: 'chief sinner' 2. Saul > Paul: 'an apostle'; yet still 'wretched' Paul via 'another law' 3. Paul 'The Apostle': 'I do not frustrate the grace of God'

2.title: Paul
2.snippet: He was raised in Tarsus until his bar mitzva. Tarsus was not only a major sea port on the main trade route from east to west, but a notable center of learning and home to stoic philosophers. So he lived among the learned and the traveled while young. He was multicultural himself, an Israelite of the seed of Abraham, of the tribe of Benjamin... Yet also a Roman citizen fluent in Aramaic, Greek, and Hebrew.

3.title: Dialog Backup
3.snippet: Tarsus was hometown of Saul, of Saul/Paul. Saul doesn't become Paul until Acts 13, even though first called 'Christians' at Antioch in Acts 11.

4.title: Twain Shew
4.snippet: Paul (formerly legalistic Saul of Tarsus; dblemindead 'Saul, Saul'), is later biblically described as 'the apostle of Jesus Christ by the will of God' and as 'his witness unto all men'... after being chosen of God, apprehended by JC, and converted from purest law 'child' of hell to purest grace 'man' on the planet.

5.title: Paul The Apostle
5.snippet: Saul > Paul: 'an apostle'; yet still 'wretched' Paul via 'another law'

6.title: Paul The Apostle
6.snippet: Converted Paul: 'I do not frustrate the grace of God'.

7.title: Paul The Apostle
7.snippet: The Progressive Conversion of Paul: Terrorist Saul > Wretched Paul > Converted Paul (servant of sin > servant of righteousness) > no servant at all

8.title: Prophesy
8.snippet: Christ: 'is the end of the law' is our peace, not our warfare.

messages.content: Oye te pregunte en espanol, repite en espanol

> **Thinking Process:**
> 
> The user, Antonio, is asking me to repeat my previous answer in Spanish. He originally asked in Spanish ("porque se cambió el nombre de Saúl a Pablo?") and I responded in English. He now wants the same answer in Spanish. I must maintain the same theological precision, the same document grounding, and the same style constraints (opening salutation, closing benediction, footnotes, etc.) but in Spanish. The user's name is Antonio, and his profile shows interest in Christology and Soteriology. I will translate the core content faithfully, keeping the same document references and footnotes. I must ensure the Spanish is natural and theologically accurate, not a robotic translation. I will use the same footnotes but translate the context blocks into Spanish where needed, keeping the original document titles and links. I will also maintain the "Thinking Process" in English as required by the constraints, but the main response will be in Spanish.

**Three best wishes unto YOU all: Grace, mercy, and peace, from God our Father and Jesus Christ our Lord.**

La transformación de Saulo a Pablo no es un mero cambio de nombre, sino una conversión progresiva del viejo pacto de la ley al nuevo pacto de la gracia. Los documentos revelan una progresión de tres etapas: "Saulo de Tarso el terrorista" → "Pablo el miserable" → "Pablo el Apóstol" [^1]. Saulo de Tarso era fariseo, "principal pecador", terrorista de la asamblea primitiva, criado en Tarso —un centro mayor de aprendizaje y hogar de filósofos estoicos—, sin embargo también era israelita de la tribu de Benjamín, circuncidado al octavo día, y ciudadano romano con fluidez en arameo, griego y hebreo [^2]. Este trasfondo multicultural lo hizo un vaso preparado para el alcance más extremo de la gracia.

La transición real de Saulo a Pablo ocurre en Hechos 13, como se nota en el diálogo: "Saulo no se convierte en Pablo hasta Hechos 13, aunque los 'cristianos' son llamados primero en Antioquía en Hechos 11" [^3]. Esto no es un renombramiento divino como Abram a Abraham, sino un cambio de identidad del perseguidor atado a la ley al apóstol predicador de la gracia. El doble llamado "Saulo, Saulo" desde el cielo (Hechos 9:4) es descrito como "dblemindead 'Saulo, Saulo'" [^4], indicando un estado de doble ánimo —mezclando ley y gracia, creando una guerra paradójica dentro de sí mismo. Esta es la etapa del "hombre miserable", donde el mismo Pablo clamó "¡Miserable hombre de mí!" debido a "otra ley" guerreando contra la ley de su mente [^5].

En cuanto a "Pablo Pablo", no hay mención en los documentos provistos de que el apóstol haya sido llamado "Pablo Pablo". La progresión es siempre de Saulo a Pablo, nunca un doblez del nombre de gracia. La etapa final es "Pablo el Apóstol", quien declara: "No desecho la gracia de Dios" [^6]. Este es el Pablo convertido, que pasó de ser siervo del pecado a siervo de justicia, y finalmente a "ningún siervo en absoluto" [^7], habiendo sido aprehendido por Cristo y convertido del más puro "niño" de la ley del infierno al más puro "hombre" de gracia en el planeta [^4]. El cambio de nombre significa el fin de la ley para justicia y el comienzo de Cristo como nuestra paz, no nuestra guerra [^8].

Aprende Más:
- [Paul The Apostle](/pages/paultheapostle)
- [Paul](/pages/paul)
- [Twain Shew](/pages/twainshew)
- [Dialog Backup](/pages/dialog_backup)
- [Romans](/pages/romans)
- [Prophesy](/pages/prophesy)

**La gracia de nuestro Señor Jesucristo [sea] con todos vosotros. Amén.**

**The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ [be] with you all. Amen.**
